videos.description:
Split is located in western Croatia along the Dalmatian coast. As Croatia’s second largest city, it’s always buzzing with life and has been for thousands of years. Split was put on the map by the emperor Diocletian, who had his palace built here between 295 and 305. After his death, the palace was continuously used throughout history by different civilizations, like the Romans, Byzantines, and Venetians. Today, life continues between the old city walls and it’s one of the reasons why travelers visit. But Split’s unique setting between the dramatic coastal mountains and the turquoise waters of the Adriatic Sea also make it an ideal vacation hub. In this video, we’ll highlight 10 amazing things to do in and around Split.

videos.transcript: What's up guys, we're here in Split, Croatia. In this video, we're going to show you the best things to do in around the city so hope you enjoy it! Split is located in Western Croatia along the Dalmatian Coast. As Croatia's second-largest city, it's always buzzing with life, and has been for thousands of years. Split was put on the map by emperor Diocletian, who had his palace built there between 295 and 305. After his death, the palace was continuously used throughout history by different civilizations, like the Romans, Byzantines, and Venetians. Today, life continues between the old city walls and is one of the reasons why travelers visit. But Split's unique setting between the dramatic coastal mountains and the turquoise waters of the Adriatic Sea also make it an ideal vacation hub. In this video, we'll highlight 10 amazing things to do in and around Split. So let's dive in. We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. The lively waterfront promenade, known as the 'riva', is the best central reference point in Split. Lined with cafes and restaurants, it's a great place to eat and drink or just stroll around and breathe in the atmosphere. It can get busy sometimes because of the immense cruise ships that dock here almost on a daily basis. Diocletian's Palace is one of the most impressive ancient Roman monuments in existence today, and you'll likely spend most of your time in Split here. Located in front of the harbor, it's the beating heart of the city and you'll find plenty of bars, restaurants, and shops inside its walls. In fact, there are over 200 buildings within the palace boundaries. And even though the original structure has been added to and altered continuously over millennia, it's absolutely fascinating to walk through its narrow streets. Access this imposing structure from one of its four historic gates. Located inside the palace, Split's Cathedral is one of the best-preserved ancient Roman buildings still standing. It was originally built as a mausoleum to Diocletian. However, in the 5th Century, the Christians destroyed the Emperor's sarcophagus and converted his tomb into a church dedicated to one of his victims. You can buy a ticket that combines a visit to the cathedral with the Bell Tower, which offers a panoramic view of the city. It also includes the Treasury, where you can find preserved sculptures and paintings that relate to the cathedral's history, as well as the Temple of Jupiter, which is an ancient Roman Temple that has been converted into the cathedral's baptistry. So we're inside the Temple of Jupiter and there's actually a lot of cool decorations here. And you can also see these facial expressions actually in the ceiling here. And it's really beautiful, like there's a lot of them. To the west of Split loom's Marjan Forest Park, a nature reserve with a wealth of walking trails. The most popular one passes shortly through a pine forest before reaching a scenic lookout of Split and the surrounding islands. It's a great place to watch the sunset from. But if you need a true escape from the city, you can also continue through the park to other viewpoints and even a beach. The park is big enough that you could spend an entire afternoon here if you want. We put a graphic on the screen here, for how to get from Riva Promenade to the main viewpoint on Marjan Hill if you want to do that for sunset. Split is more than just a palace and you'll find fascinating buildings and monuments all around its downtown area. Get lost in its narrow streets, go shopping, or try some local cuisine. But the best way to learn about this fascinating city is to go on a guided walking tour. Not only will you visit Split's main highlights, but also become familiar with the culture and the way of life here. Find a link to a guided tour of Split and Diocletian's Palace in the description below. One of Croatia's absolute highlights is only an hour drive from Split. Krka National Park extends along the 73 kilometer or 50 mile Krka river, which runs through a karstic canyon. It's a breathtaking area, filled with waterfalls and gorges that you can explore on foot via boardwalks and trails. The park actually has five main entrances, which are all accessible by car but located far from one another. Skradinski Buk is the highlight of the park, where you can take a beautiful walk on the emerald green river and end at the park's most impressive waterfall. But Roški Slap, Burnum, and Manojlovac are also worth a visit. If you don't have a car, you can also book a day tour here, and we left a link for that in the description. Located 12 kilometers or 7.4 miles to the northeast of downtown Split, you can find the impressive Klis Fortress. This fort offers beautiful views of the surrounding valley and the Adriatic Sea. Inside, you can explore the various fortifications and visit a small museum with details of the castle's past. If you're a Game of Thrones fan, you'll also recognize the Klis Fortress as Meereen, which appeared in season 4 and 5. There's a room inside the fort with pictures to freshen up your memory. And alternatively, you can also visit the Game of Thrones Museum in the city center of Split. Croatia has hundreds of islands dotting its coastline and those near Split are some of the most pristine on the entire Dalmatian Coast. Because of their close proximity to the mainland, some islands can be discovered on an island-hopping tour from Split. We joined the 5 Island Tour during which we snorkeled and swam in the pristine waters of the Blue Lagoon, visited charming villages on the islands of Vis and Hvar, and explored the dazzling Blue Cave. We cruised on a speedboat, allowing for a smaller group and more intimate experience. So we made it to the Blue Lagoon here. We're about to go take a quick swim, and we thought this is a good time to mention the sponsor of today's video. GetYourGuide is a platform where you can book unforgettable travel experiences. They actually offer 60,000 different experiences from destinations all around the world. And one of the cool things is that the guides are always locals, so they have a lot of different knowledge about the history of the area, and just some local insights for you. In our opinion, GetYourGuide is really the best platform right now to pre-book your travel activities because the App is super easy to use and you can have your ticket right on your phone, you don't have to print anything out. And there's 24/7 support, there's free cancellation 24 hours in advance if you need to. There's also sometimes skip-the-line options. So we highly recommend GetYourGuide. There's a link to the tour that we did today in the description below along with a special discount code and there's links to all the other tours that are in the Split area, so I hope you enjoy it. If you're looking for a more relaxed atmosphere but equally beautiful medieval walls, then you should add Trogir to your Split itinerary. Located only 30 kilometers from the city center of Split, the old town of Trogir is set on a tiny Island linked by bridges to the mainland as well as the neighboring larger island. Within its walls, you can find many beautiful buildings that were built between the 12th and 15th century. Walk its narrow alleyways and the beautiful boulevard that's lined with cafes, restaurants, and yachts. Last but not least, we have the world famous Plitvice Lakes. Known for its large network of karstic emerald lakes and stunning waterfalls, this park is a must-visit when in Croatia and is protected by UNESCO for a good reason. You can visit the park as a day trip from Split and we left a link for that tour in the description. However, it's definitely easier to visit this magical place from the closer city of Zadar, which is where we did it from. Check out our Zadar video to get the full details about Plitvice Lakes. If you're planning your Croatia trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code below. We also included important links that you'll need for your Split trip in the description, such as a full travel guide with where to stay and where to eat. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

videos.transcript: Want to learn more than just what to DO in Split Croatia to plan your trip? Great! Because this one-stop shop travel guide comes firsthand from my Split solo travel to get you ready - covering essential tips like best time to go, where to stay, how to get around, day trips, budget tips, and MORE. Traveling beyond Croatia? Check out all of CORR Travel's videos and Subscribe to get the best in travel guides, itineraries, and tips that, yep, all come from my actual travel. All right, let's get you ready for your unforgettable vacation in Split. [Music] Why visit Split? Croatia's second largest city, Split's start hails from the establishment of Diocletian's Palace in the 4th century by the Roman Emperor Diocletian that today rests in the heart of Old Town and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This ancient city's history and Roman ruins beg to be explored. The Croatian's knowledge and enthusiasm of their home is infectious. Ask any local about Split and they'll proudly regale you with historical facts and stories, not to mention great recommendations on what to do in Split. Nestled between mountains and the Adriatic's crystal clear waters, Split's stunning landscape provides an abundance of land and water activities, a jumping off point to explore local islands, and some of the best beaches in Europe, as well as incredible cuisine and wine - as the locals like to point out the region is the origin of the Zinfandel grape. I LOVE Split. Trust me, you'll be planning your visit back to this Dalmatian coastal city before you even leave. Time of year, holidays, and events affect the best time to visit. Split's year- round temperatures average from the high 30s to high 80s Fahrenheit. Peak Season months are June through August that have the warmest air and water temperatures, the least amount of rain, and longest days, but also have the most tourists causing crowded beaches and higher travel costs. Low Season months are November through March, which have the coldest air and water temperatures with the shortest days but also have the fewest tourist and lowest travel costs. Shoulder Season months are April, May, September, and October, which are the best time to travel due to long days, warm water and weather, and fewer tourists for more space on the beach and lower travel costs than Peak Season. Festivals and events happen year-round and can impact costs, so check Split's schedule when planning your itinerary. Number 3: Getting to Split. All flights arrive at the Split Airport (SPU), which has one terminal, and is 23 km from the Split City Center. Split has two train stations: the Predgradje and the Split Main Train Station, which is the most convenient being located close to all fery departure and arrival points, and a 5-minute walk to Old Town. Buses arrive at the Autobusni kolodvor, or "Main Bus Station", next to the Main Train Station. The largest passenger port in Croatia, multiple cruise line ships arrive Split every day and dock at the Split Riva pier making it busy, so plan plenty of time for your arrival and departures. And 29 international, domestic and ferry routes pass through Split's ferry port for both passenger and car ferries. To get around Split, let's start with getting from the Split Airport to the city center. Without a hotel shuttle, the quickest and most convenient way is by vehicle. Uber and Bolt ride shares pick up at the airport main road next to the public bus stop, and cost about €25 to €30. Taxis are at the entrance and cost about €30, and both take about 25 minutes to reach the city center, depending on traffic. Private Transfers could cost the same or more, but can be arranged online in advance. See some links to Private Transfers below for your convenience. The blue, Pleso Prevoz airport shuttle is €6, paid online or to the driver. It runs 7:30 a.m. to 11:00 p.m. and takes about 30 minutes to reach the Main Split Bus Station. The number 37 public bus costs only €2.50, but it's not as convenient as the airport shuttle. Water Taxi rides are 20 minutes to the city center, but it's a 10-minute walk to the taxi dock and cost about €120. And you can rent a car at the airport, but it's really not worth the cost or trying to find parking. Now in the city center, let's talk about getting around the city center. Split is VERY walkable with many of the the top things to do accessible on foot, and the Old Town and Palace are comprised of narrow, stone, pedestrian streets filled with restaurants, bars, hotels, and shopping. A large portion of Split is flat, so you can explore outside the Old Town by bike, e-bike, scooter, or Segway. The public bus network is extensive and runs daily from 5:00 a.m. to midnight making it another great way to go beyond Old Town. Buy a ticket for the entire day and explore the parks, historical sites, beaches, and more. Serviced by eight companies, Split ferries go to 26 destinations making it easy to explore the surrounding islands like Hvar, Brač, and Korčula, full of history, culture, food, wine, AND more beaches and water activities. You can explore Split and beyond using any of these methods self-guided, or learn more about Split and the region while meeting other travelers by participating in a food, wine, or historic walking, biking, e-biking, Segway, or island tour, all of which are fun at any age. I have links to some of the BEST rated tours in the video description below to get you started. Number 5: Safety Tips. Split's crime rate is very low and I never felt unsafe walking alone day or night as a female, solo traveler. Still, always just practice general safety precautions like: walking in well-lit areas and keeping your belongings on you at all times, with bag pockets closed, and no cash or valuables in your pockets, especially in crowded or touristy areas; drink water, stay hydrated, and keep alcohol consumption at a moderate level, especially if you expect to be diving or doing other water activities; wear sunscreen always in the Shoulder and Peak Season months; and, don't stay out on unpopulated beaches alone or past sunset, bring only essentials with you, and never leave them unattended. Other than that, Split is considered one of the safest places to travel. Make your Croatia travel as easy easy as possible and bring THESE travel essentials: a VPN service for data security; portable Wi-Fi, or get a Croatia SIM card; a solar charger so your devices are always charged on the beach, boating trips, or other excursions; a reusable water bottle to fill up at the tap, to stay hydrated, reduce costs and waste; a hat for any time of year; comfortable walking shoes for walking, hiking, and the Croatian pebble beaches; sunglasses with UV protection; a mono-sling bag to keep your valuable safe and stay handsfree while traveling; a waterproof waste pouch to keep your items with you in the water, not unattended on the beach; and, a thermal phone case to keep your mobile device from overheating in the sun. I have links to some of these travel essentials for you in the video description below for your convenience. Number 7: Where to Stay. The Split city center, or "Old Town", is where it's at for first-time stay for quick access to must-do attractions, dining, night life, and transportation to outside the city. Visiting Split, I look for a central location for easy walking, good price, amenities, free cancellation, breakfast, overall ratings, and a Travel Sustainable rating, sustainable certification, or, at minimum, a variety of sustainable practices. Checking all of these boxes AND more, my top hotel choices to consider in Split are: Cornaro Hotel, Jupiter Luxury Hotel, Heritage hotel Santa Lucia, Hotel Slavija, and Starlight Luxury Rooms. I stayed at Starlight Luxury Rooms on my last trip to Split. Conveniently located close to Diocletian's Palace, I had a large, comfortable room and bed, an incredible concierge, nice breakfast, AND a reasonable price. I would DEFINITELY stay there again. Going the hostel route with the same great options, in either a private or dorm- style room, try: Hostel Split or Hostel Dvor. Links to all of these great accommodations are available for you in the video description below. Number 8: Top Things to Do! Split has MUCH to offer, so all sites and activities can't be mentioned here; but, here are definitely SOME of the top things to see and do in and around Split: [Music] Yes, there's more. Don't forget to put these, at a minimum, on your Split travel itinerary. Split's Adriatic location and long history is reflected in its cuisine, with Italian, Turkish, and Hungarian influences offering a diverse culinary experience. Seafood is a staple in Split, so don't miss the dishes made from skampi, muscles, crab, and octopus, often served with sides of spaghetti, gnocchi, or risotto. Sample the local cheeses, Dalmatian prosciutto, fresh olives, and of course, dipping fresh breads into the delicious Croatian olive oil. Dine with, or just sip, Dalmatia’s renowned wines like whites made from the Pošip grape, or luscious reds like the Plavac Mali. For dessert, try powdered sugar fritters called “fritule”, or, well, whatever satisfies your sweet tooth. Split is blessed with a plethora of dining locations. Beaches have cafes and bars so you can lounge all day without having to go anywhere. Fresh produce, like the local grapes and figs, are a must-try at the Green Market. For authentic Dalmatian dining and rustic charm, hit the local konobas. Diocletian's Palace and Old Town's streets hold wine cellars, bars, and an eclectic mix of outdoor cafes and bistros for a blend of Mediterranean and international flavors. For seafood specialties with a picturesque Adriatic view, dine at any of the Riva promenade restaurants. There's a lot to try. Wander the streets to read menus or just ask your concierge, waiter, or a local for their recommendations. And add in food and drink, and culture tours with tastings, to help mix it up. When the sun sets, Split's nightlife scene ignites. The lively Riva promenade has trendy bars and cafes for sipping cocktails by the water, as well as beachside venues and night clubs with DJs for dancing. Although not customary in Split, it's good to check to see if your bill has a tip included; and, if not, you may round up the bill to the next euro or tip around 5% for casual dining, or up to 15% for fine dining, if you feel you had good service. Always tip in cash, which you can just leave on the table. Split will inspire you to want to see MORE of Croatia. Trust me! Fortunately, Split is ideal for participating in half and full day excursions and tours to enjoy more cuisine, natural landscapes, history and culture. You can start with any of the surrounding islands, such as Hvar or Brač with the renowned Golden Horn Beach. Or, go beyond with a day trip to Trogir or Krka National Park. Also doable are full day trips to Plitvice Lakes National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, or Dubrovnik. There are more day trip locations to choose from. Many can be done self-guided, by private tour, or group tour, which make it easier to see more of Croatia while meeting other travelers. I have GREAT, highly-rated day trip tour links for you below to get you started planning. Number 11: Budget Tips. Split is not as expensive to travel as some other European cities. Still, if you're traveling Split on a budget, keep the following in mind while booking and traveling: Visit in the Shoulder or Off-Season months; travel during the week, avoiding holidays and large events; book your accommodations very early, and/or stay outside the city center still within walking distance or close to a bus stop; depending on time of year, location, and amenities, a hostel dorm room can start at €15, and a private room at €45 a night; take the airport shuttle bus to and from the airport; avoid dining in high tourist areas, like Old Town, by shopping at a local market and booking a room with a refrigerator; purchase a Croatia or Europe eSIM or SIM card instead of a costly international plan through your local carrier; bring cash to avoid the pricey ATM fees; and, take advantage of Split's beaches, parks, and free historical and cultural sites. Travel Croatia with your eco-friendly foot forward, and the planet in mind, by using any of these EASY, eco-travel tips that DO make a difference: Look for flights that emit less CO2 through your airline or through Skyscanner's Greener Flight filter; purchase carbon offsets through your airline, or third party, to lower your flight's carbon footprint; the less you pack, the lighter the plane, and the less fuel it uses, plus traveling carry-on saves time in the airport; in addition to Booking.com's Travel Sustainable properties, Green Globe and Green Key Global also have eco-friendly certified lodging options; during all phases of travel, including on a food tour, reduce your plastic trash and your carbon footprint by bringing your own reusable shopping bags, water bottle, and bamboo travel utensils, which take up VERY little space in your carry-on bag; and, bring marine-safe sunscreen with you to protect the Adriatic marine life. Number 13... If you're still with me, here are your valuable, PRO travel tips just for Split: Croatia has switched from the kuna to the euro; the water IS safe to drink in Split; Croatia's beaches are pebbly, not sandy, many beaches will have eateries, cafes or bars, outdoor showers, restrooms, and lounge chairs and umbrellas for rental, but we'll go quickly in the Peak Season; Split has free Wi-Fi in the airport, public buses, and in many public areas and beaches; heads up - there are some nude beaches there; some places only accept cash, so bring plenty with you; the locals are VERY friendly and helpful - ask them for recommendations on anything to do or avoid; drink plenty of water in the Peak and warmer Shoulder Season months; the best time to visit Diocletian's Palace is first thing in the morning during Peak Season to avoid the crowds; many smaller establishments and activity offices use WhatsApp for communicating and notifying you of any scheduling changes; and, locals may speak English; however, it's common courtesy the lead a conversation in Croatian. If they speak English in return, that is a courtesy. So, how much time DO you need in Split? The amount of time to spend depends on your interests and activities you wish to undertake. Split's Old Town is compact, making it possible to see attractions and scenery in one day. However, for a fuller mix of historical sites, beaches, water sports, cultural and dining experiences, and adding day trips in a relaxing manner, a week in Split would be ideal. If you don't have a week, however, I recommend spending a MINIMUM of 3 days in Split to be able to engage in the local top things to do with at least one day trip. videos.transcript: Split is known for its Roman ancient history, including the impressive Diolesian Palace. So, if you're planning to visit, stay tuned because in today's video, I'll unveil the top 10 things you need to know to help you plan your trip. If we haven't met yet, I'm Kim and I specialize in crafting short travel guides on popular destinations. So, if you're passionate about traveling, be sure to check out my playlist on different cities and join the community so you don't miss out on your next bucket list adventure. Number 10, best time to visit. Split has a Mediterranean climate with hot, dry summers and mild winters. The best time to visit is just before peak season in May or June or right after from September to mid-occtober. During these months, you'll enjoy warm sunny days, ideal for both sightseeing and beach time without the summer crowds. Prices, especially for accommodations, can be two to three times lower than in July and August, while restaurants and activities are typically around 30% less expensive. Number nine, must visit attractions. Splits historic center is built directly inside the occlesian palace, a UNESCO world heritage site where ancient Roman walls now surround restaurants, shops, boutique hotels, and local homes. Inside you'll find landmarks like the Paris style courtyard, the Cathedral of St. Dominus with its underground crypt and the bell tower which offers panoramic views of the city and the sea. As for museums, I recommend the archaeological museum which features Roman statues and artifacts and the Maritime Museum filled with ship models and exhibits from Croatia's maritime past. Number eight, getting around the city. Split is a pedestrianfriendly city and most major landmarks like the old town, the prominad, the port, and nearby beaches are all within a 10 to 15 minute walk. If you're flying in, you can take the airport shuttle for about €8, the public bus for around €3, or an Uber, which is usually cheaper and more reliable than a taxi. For places just outside the center like Margin Hill or Benny Beach, local buses are your best option since Split doesn't have a tram or metro system. Number seven, rent a car. If you're planning to explore beyond Split, renting a car is a great idea. It gives you the freedom to visit national parks, nearby towns like Trugier or even take a scenic drive along the coast to the Bravik. I personally booked through discover cars which compares major rental sites. At the end of May, I paid just €10 a day plus €5 for full insurance. When booking, I recommend choosing companies with at least an A+ rating, filtering for automatic cars if needed since they're harder to find in Croatia, and booking early to get the best rate. I've added the link below if you want to check it out. Number six, local cuisine and dining etiquette. Split food is simple, fresh, and full of Mediterranean flavor. You'll definitely want to try black risoto made with cuddlefish ink, pastichada, and of course, burk, a flaky pastry filled with cheese or meat, which was definitely my favorite. Dinner usually starts around 8:00 p.m., and meals are meant to be slow and social. Some restaurants include a service charge, but if not, tipping is appreciated and most people leave between five and 10% of the bill. Number five, best beaches to visit. Split has a great mix of beaches. The most popular is Bagviche Beach, just minutes from the city center with shallow waters and lively bars. Ideal for young travelers or anyone looking for a social scene. If you're looking for something quieter, follow the coastal path from the Marina to Ojina Beach. A rocky spot near Margin Hill that's dog friendly, has plenty of parking, and offers a more laid-back vibe. Further west, Kazuni Beach is one of Split's most iconic spots with stunning views, a beach club, and sunbeds available to rent for about €35 a day. Finally, if you're visiting as a family, check out Benny Beach. It's more relaxed, shaded by pine trees, and has a casual local restaurant right by the water. All beaches outside Bev Viche, are ideal for sunset and easy to reach from the city center by bus 12, which costs just €2 each way. Number four, explore beyond the city. Split makes a great base for day trips, both by land and sea. The Blue Cave is one of the most popular boat tours, and I recommend choosing a ride that's comfortable as you'll be on the water for several hours. Also popular are island hopping tours, which generally include three to five stops with destinations like the Blue Lagoon, Var, Pra, and Sulta. If you'd rather stay on land, you can visit the ancient ruins of Salona or the UNESCO listed town of Truge, both easy to reach by public bus. Other favorites of mine include rafting on the Satina River, catching the sunset from Cliss Fortress and visiting Kirka National Park to see the waterfalls and stroll along the wooden trails. I've linked my curated list of experiences below if you'd like to check them out. Number three, fairies, catamarans, and highland hopping. Split is one of the main departure points for fairies in Croatia, making highland hopping incredibly easy. The port sits right next to the old town with daily departures to islands like Var, Pra, and Sulta. Fairies are slower but more affordable with passenger tickets starting around €6 and they often arrive at smaller ports outside the main towns. Kamarans on the other hand are faster passenger only and usually take you straight into the highlands main towns. You can book tickets online or directly at the terminal, but in July and August, it's best to book in advance as sailings often sell out. Number two, currency and payment. Croatia uses the euro and most places accept credit and debit cards. That said, it's still a good idea to carry some cash, especially for markets, beach bars, and parking meters, which often require coins. Stick to ATMs from Croatian banks to avoid extra fees, and let your bank know you're traveling to prevent any card blocks. Number one, safety and local etiquette. Croatia has been ranked the safest country in Europe for three years in a row and Split reflects that. It's common to see women walking alone at night and travelers generally feel very safe here even after dark. Most people in Split speak English, but making an effort to communicate in their language is always appreciated. You can greet people with doardan for good day and mimm for please or you're welcome. One thing to keep in mind, swimwear and shirtless outfits aren't allowed in the city center and can lead to fines. videos.transcript: Split is known for its Roman roots showcasing ancient architecture like the occlesian's palace. So, if you're planning to visit Croatia's second largest city, stay tuned because in today's video, I'm sharing my top 10 recommendations of things to do to help you plan your trip. For those new to my channel, I'm Kim and I specialize in crafting short travel guides on popular destinations. So, if you're passionate about travel, be sure to check out my playlist on various cities and join the community so you don't miss out on your next bucket list adventure. Number 10, join a boat tour. One of the best ways to explore the Croatian coast is by joining a full-day boat tour. First, there's the famous Blue Cave, a speedboat trip that takes you to five islands, including Vis Havar, the glowing blue cave itself, and even a Fumamia filming location. Just note that the entrance fee to the Blue Cave isn't always included, and it ranges from 12 to€24 depending on the season. For something more laid-back, try a Tree Islands tour to the Blue Lagoon. Perfect for swimming and snorkeling, these cruises often include lunch, drinks, and music with extra stops at nearby beaches or islands. Number nine, food tour. Split offers several local food tours, and I joined one that combined a guided walk through the old town with stops at five local spots. most of them familyrun and loved by locals. Our first stop featured Dalmatian procuto, local cheese, olive oil and homemade bread. This was followed by black risoto and pastichada as well as bere a flaky pastry filled with cheese or meat. Finally, we wrapped up the tour with a refreshing gelato. And trust me, you won't leave hungry. I've linked my curated list of experiences below if you'd like to check them out. Number eight, Kirka National Park. From Split, you've got two national parks within reach. Kirka, just 1 and 1/2 hours away, and Plevitz Lakes, which is a bit more remote, around 3 and 1/2 hours by bus. If you're visiting Kirka, you'll follow a wooden trail passing scenic cascades along the way, all leading to one of the most photogenic spots in Croatia, the park's most iconic waterfall. When booking, you can choose a tour that includes bonus stops like the medieval town of Troier or a local wine tasting, adding something special to the journey. Number seven, visit a winery. If you're into wine, Split has two standout experiences I personally tried. The first was a familyowned vineyard passed down for over a,000 years. There you'll get to walk through the vines and enjoy your first glass while learning about the winery's history. Afterward, you'll head into the cellar for two more tastings, then choose your favorite to pair with local cheeses and meats as the registation continues. For something more curated, I visited Jardine just outside Split. This experience blends refined fusion dishes with custom wine pairings crafted by the owner himself. Overall, it's a slower, more artistic take on Dalmatian flavor, and they also offer cooking classes if you want to dive deeper. Number six, best beaches to visit. Split has a great mix of beaches and the most popular is Bagviche Beach, just minutes from the city center with shallow waters and lively bars. It's ideal for young travelers or anyone looking for a fun social scene. For something quieter, follow the coastal path from the Marina to Oenna Beach, a rocky dog friendly spot near Margin Hill with plenty of parking and a laid-back vibe. Further west is Kazuni Beach, one of Split's most iconic seaside spots, where you'll find a beach club and sunbeds for rent. Typically around €35 a day. A little further down the coast, Benny Beach offers a shaded, familyfriendly atmosphere with pine trees, sunbeds, and a casual local restaurant by the water. All beaches outside Bviche are great for sunset and easily reachable by bus 12 from the city center. Number five, cooking class. One of my favorite experiences was a hands-on cooking class that dives into Mediterranean cuisine. I joined one that started with a walk through the green market to pick up fresh vegetables, followed by a visit to the fish market to choose the catch of the day. Then we made our way to the cooking studio where the real fun began. We kicked things off with an appetizer and a welcome shot of local liquor and got to work. With the help of our chef, we learned how to cook risoto, prepare fresh fish, fry calamari, bake homemade bread, and cook seasonal vegetables properly. We wrapped up the class with a glass of local wine and sat down together to enjoy our creations. A must for travelers who love to cook, eat, and connect. Number four, take a sunset tour. Split is famous for its incredible sunsets, and there are two standout ways to experience them. First, hop on the city sightseeing bus for a scenic ride up to Cliss Fortress. Once you arrive, it's a short walk to the top where you'll get amazing views of the city and mountains. If you'd rather be on water, Split offers plenty of sunset cruises. I joined a pirate style ship that sailed past Margin Hill and the beaches with music, drinks, and a golden hourglow. Number three, The Ullesian Palace. With more than 1700 years of history, this UNESCO World Heritage site is now the living heart of Split. Inside the palace walls, you'll find restaurants, boutique hotels, shops, and museums, all nestled within the original Roman layout. From Paris Square, you can stop by the mainet ticket office and grab a combo ticket, which saves you money and includes access to several key landmarks, including the Cathedral of St. Dominus and its underground crypt, the Temple of Jupiter, the Treasury, and of course, the bell tower. And if you want to dive deeper, the substructure museum beneath the palace offers a fascinating look at Split's Roman foundations. Number two, the bell tower. Known as one of Split's most iconic landmarks, the bell tower was originally built to accompany the cathedral and serve as a watchtowwer over the city. To reach the top, you'll climb around 200 narrow stone and metal steps, but the effort is absolutely worth it. At the summit, you'll be rewarded with panoramic views of Splits red rooftops, the Adriatic Sea, and the surrounding mountains. To avoid the midday heat and the crowds, try visiting early in the morning or just before sunset when the light turns golden and the breeze makes the climb more enjoyable. Number one, visit a local market. Start with the Pazar or green market just east of the Ulesian Palace. You'll find fresh produce, cured meats, and local olive oil, all sold by friendly vendors. You'll also find clothing stalls and souvenirs from nearby islands like lavender products and beach essentials such as hats and water shoes. If you're planning to cook or just want to see what locals are eating, the fish market is just a few minutes away. You'll find the fresh catch of the day brought in straight from the Adriatic from sardines and seabbass to squid and octopus. Take note that most vendors accept cash only, so be sure to bring some euros with you when you visit. As always, if you like this video, please give it a big thumbs up, subscribe to my channel, and be sure to turn on the notification bell so you won't miss any of my travel guides and adventures in Split, as well as other exciting destinations. Keep on traveling, and I will see you guys in the next video. [Music]

videos.title: How to Spend a Day in Split - Our 4 Minute Port Guide
videos.description:
Split is one of the principle tourist destinations in Croatia and is a popular port stop for those on an Adriatic Cruise

With is accessible history and picture postcard views this bustling city split has so much to offer visitors. So much that it’s hard to pick what to do if you only have 1 day there.

The cruise dock is a 5-10 minute walk from the city centre. As you walk you’ll see signs for the beach - These take you away from the main route into the city but it is only 5 min walk to the nearest Bacvice Beach which is a small but pleasant Sandy beach in a sheltered bay.

Continuing into Split you 1st encounter the Riva promenade. This is a glorious wide street styled in napoleonic times filled with bars and restaurants and is the social heart of the city.

Adjacent to the Riva is a market place which is big enough to while away 30 mins hunting for bargain souvenirs and tasting local foods

Split is famed for the Diocletian Palace which is remarkably intact palace built and occupied by the Roman emperor Diocletian

This is a vast complex and it occupied most of what is currently the footprint of the old city area. There is plenty to see but after leaving the market you can enter the palace ruins via the impressive silver gate, one of several city gates to discover as your wander through this wonderous place.

The peristyle was the Palace’s central square, where powerful Roman citizens gathered or came to worship Diocletian. It is one of the best preserved parts of the Palace, an open rectangular court decorated with a colonnade. It also featured in Game of Thrones

During the summer months at 12 noon local reenactors put on a display for visitors.!The emporer and his wife are accompanied by the pratorean guard greet and receive the adulation of the crowd. It’s quite a sight

The guards March through the city after which is fabulous

The adjacent Cathedral of Saint Dominus was originally Diocletian’s Mausoleum. It was built in 305 A.D, and Diocletian was interred there after his death in 311 A.D. However Christians in the 5th century destroyed his tomb and converted it into a cathedral dedicated to one of the emperor’s victims whom he had decapitated for being a Christian . It’s considered the oldest cathedral in the world.

You can also climb the bell tower which gives a view across the city and it is also possible to access the cellars of the palace which house remains of the emperor baths

The city is great to explore on foot and is full of narrow alleys and courtyards. There are museums a plenty rainging the expected art and archeology to the somewhat bizarre

The shops are excellent and to satisfy your hunger why not try a Burek a local pastry filled with meat, potatoes, cheese and spinach .. very tasty … chased down with an ice cream of course … can’t avoid putting on a few pounds on this holiday !!

So it’s great to walk off those calories. Progressing through the city you can pass through People’s Square and Republic Square which has been modelled on St Marks square in Venice

A popular walk to burn off this calories is to climb Marjam hill. Or Holy hill as it is sometimes referred to. This is actually a large accessible nature reserve that includes a botanical garden, zoo, ancient and historic churches and some walking trails that take you to spectacular viewpoints .. it’s a good climb up the hill but we’ll worth the effort . It’s not busy or crowded and it’s easy to find peace in this magnificent place. The size and extent of the park only reveals itself when you are up there and it extends over an entire peninsula . You could easily spend an entire day exploring this natural wilderness sat on the doorstep of the main city centre

Split was a real delight and far exceeded our expectations. It’s has too much to see in a day and if you have limited time there it’s a hard choice to select the best option for you.

We absolutely loved Marjam hill .. it’s a hiker’s dream. So that’s that then another holiday to Croatia added to our ever increasing list of holiday plans.

Thank you Split ! That was the last thing we wanted to do having discovered what delights you offer visitors. We will be back.

videos.transcript: Visitors to Split are often captivated by its stunning seafront location, where azure waters meet rugged mountains. The city's Mediterranean climate ensures warm summers and mild winters, making it an ideal base for year round exploration. Split strategic position also makes it a perfect starting point for numerous day trips, allowing travelers to experience the surrounding region's diverse landscapes and cultural treasures. Krka National Park, a natural wonderland located just over an hour's drive from Split, beckons visitors with its cascading waterfalls and lush landscapes. This pristine park, spanning 109km², is home to the Krka River, which has carved a spectacular canyon through the limestone hills, creating a series of seven stunning waterfalls. Not far from Krka National Park, Šibenik is a charming coastal gem brimming with history. Wander through its medieval streets, Visit the Unesco listed Saint Jamess Cathedral and admire the impressive fortresses overlooking the sea. With its mix of culture, stunning architecture and coastal beauty, Šibenik makes for the perfect day trip from Split, combining natural wonders with rich heritage. Trogir, a small island town just 30km from Split, is a veritable open air museum that transports visitors back in time. This Unesco World Heritage Site, often referred to as Little Venice, boasts an impressive collection of Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque architecture, all crammed within its medieval walls. A day trip from Split wouldn't be complete without exploring the nearby islands. Hvar, known as the sunniest island in Croatia, is a blend of luxurious resorts, historic towns and picturesque lavender fields. The town of Hvar, with its Venetian style architecture and hilltop fortress is a must visit destination. Travelers can wander through the town's marble streets, admire the Renaissance Cathedral, or climb to the Spanish fortress for breathtaking views of the harbor and surrounding Pakleni Islands. Just a short ferry ride from Split Brač Island makes the perfect day trip. Arrive in Supetar, a charming coastal town with seaside cafes and local vibes. Head south to Bol, the island's most famous town and don't miss Zlatni Rat Beach, Croatia's most iconic stretch of sand, known for its unique shape and turquoise waters, a must for beach lovers and adventurers alike. Nestled where the Cetina River meets the Adriatic Sea, the town of Omiš offers a perfect blend of history, natural beauty and adrenaline-pumping activities. Once a notorious pirate stronghold, Omiš now welcomes visitors with its charming old town, beautiful beaches and a wealth of outdoor adventures. The Cetina River Canyon, a natural wonder, carving its way through limestone cliffs provides the backdrop for a range of exciting activities. Thrill seekers can try their hand at whitewater rafting, navigating the river's rapids while surrounded by stunning scenery. For those seeking an even bigger adrenaline rush, the Cetina offers canyoning in a deep gorge. Nestled in the heart of the Imotski region, the blue and Red lakes are two of Croatia's most striking natural wonders. The deep blue waters of the Blue Lake shimmer against the surrounding cliffs, while the Red lake, steep walls and dramatic color make it truly unique. Explore the tranquil beauty of these phenomena and enjoy a peaceful escape, just an hour's drive from Split, surrounded by breathtaking landscapes, A day trip to Biokovo is a must for nature lovers and adventure seekers. Just a short drive from Split, this rugged mountain range offers panoramic views. Hike through its pristine landscapes, Visit the Skywalk for jaw dropping vistas, or simply enjoy the peace and tranquility of the stunning scenery. Whether you're trekking or just soaking in the views below, Biokovo is unforgettable. One hour drive from Split, the Makarska Riviera offers a perfect blend of stunning beaches, charming coastal towns and breathtaking mountain backdrops. Explore the vibrant town of Makarska, wander along the waterfront and unwind on the picturesque beaches with crystal clear waters and dramatic peaks. This hidden gem is ideal for those seeking natural beauty and relaxation in one unforgettable day. Ready to embark on your Croatian adventure? Start planning your day trips from Split today and uncover the hidden gems of the Dalmatian coast.

videos.title: How to Spend 3 Days in SPLIT Croatia | The Perfect Itinerary
videos.description:
Plan the perfect 3 days in Split with this itinerary covering must-see highlights, experiences, and travel tips.

Subscribe for detailed travel itineraries and trip planning guides 👉 https://bit.ly/3B0qTJO

🔥Best Tours & Experiences in Split 👉 https://www.viator.com/Split/d4185-ttd?pid=P00282879&mcid=42383&medium=link

TIMESTAMPS 👇
0:00 - Intro
0:47 Day 1 - Get Your Bearings
4:04 Day 2 - Adventure Day
6:34 Day 3 - Hidden Gems
9:37 - Essential Practical Information
12:06 - Mistakes to Avoid

Walk through a gate in Split's Old Town and you are not entering a historic neighbourhood — you are stepping inside a two-thousand-year-old Roman emperor's palace. The same walls Diocletian retired behind in three hundred and five AD now house restaurants, apartments, and some of the most atmospheric alleyways in Europe.
Three days here is exactly right, but only if you use them well. We are covering the palace timing trick that gives you the whole place to yourself, a hike above the city with island views, a full-day escape to Krka National Park, and the planning mistake that catches almost every first-time visitor off guard.
This is how to spend three days in Split. Let's dive in.

DAY ONE: Get Your Bearings
The most important thing to understand about Split's Old Town is that it is not a museum — it is a living city built inside the remains of a Roman palace. Roughly three thousand people live within the palace walls today, surrounded by the same stone corridors Emperor Diocletian walked in the fourth century. That context makes wandering it feel completely different from any other historic centre in Europe.
Start early. By ten-thirty in the morning, organised tour groups begin moving through the main corridors and the narrow lanes fill quickly. Aim to be inside the palace — the easiest entry is through the Brass Gate on the southern waterfront, or the Golden Gate on the north — by eight in the morning at the latest.
The centrepiece is the Peristyle, the original ceremonial courtyard of the palace. It is still open-air, still flanked by ancient columns, and at this hour it belongs almost entirely to whoever is standing in it. Just off the Peristyle is the Vestibule — a circular domed hall that once served as the formal entrance to Diocletian's private apartments — with acoustics so good that local klapa singers perform traditional a cappella music here most mornings. Listen for it. Nearby, the Cathedral of Saint Domnius is built inside Diocletian's own mausoleum, making it one of the oldest Catholic cathedrals in the world in its original structure. Entry to the cathedral complex is tiered by how many of the five sites you visit — the bell tower alone is around eight US dollars, while the all-inclusive ticket covering the cathedral, crypt, baptistery, treasury, and bell tower runs around twelve dollars. Climbing the bell tower delivers excellent rooftop views over the palace towards the Adriatic.
The Palace Cellars — the labyrinthine substructure beneath the main floor — are the most underrated stop inside the complex. Entry is around nine US dollars per adult, and the network of underground rooms gives the clearest picture of what the above-ground palace would have looked like in its original form. Daenerys used these same cellars as her throne room in Meereen during Game of Thrones, if that is useful context.
For lunch, head to the Varoš neighbourhood just west of the palace walls and look for Konoba Hvaranin on Ban Mladenova Street — a no-frills local restaurant serving traditional Dalmatian pasticada, black risotto, and fish stew at prices that feel genuinely fair for the location, with mains around twelve to twenty dollars per person.
Spend the afternoon along the Riva, Split's palm-lined waterfront promenade just south of the palace. It is the social heart of the city — locals and visitors share the same chairs, the same coffee, and the same unobstructed view of the islands sitting just offshore. Follow it west toward the Ivan Meštrović Gallery in the Meje neighbourhood, dedicated to Croatia's most celebrated sculptor, with entry around thirteen US dollars and one of the more rewarding one-hour cultural stops in the city.
After dark, the palace is at its best — the stone warm under artificial light, the Peristyle nearly empty, the restaurants and wine bars on the side streets opening their terraces. Dinner mains at restaurants just outside the palace walls typically run fifteen to twenty-eight dollars per person.
Tomorrow is for getting above the city — and the view from Marjan Hill at golden hour with the islands laid out in front of you is one of those moments that stays with you long after you have left.
